name: Resan
classification_notes: The designer explicitly cites transitional types of the 17th and early 18th centuries as the primary influence, with sharp rhythm, generous proportions and finely cut details characteristic of that period.
summary: A display serif by Karol Mularczyk drawing on transitional type from the 17th and 18th centuries, offered in five weights with matching italics and variable font support.
description: Informed by the organic warmth of transitional serifs, the design pairs sharp rhythm and generous proportions with finely cut details suited to display sizes. An alternative terminals feature allows the terminal shapes to be swapped, meaningfully shifting the overall mood of the type. The family is presented as the first chapter of a broader Resan superfamily, with additional optical weights in development.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Resan as informed by the characteristics of transitional types of the 17th and early 18th centuries, bringing organic warmth and timeless elegance to a precise, regular design. Currently offered in 10 Display styles, they frame it as the beginning of the Resan journey, with further optical weights in the works to complete the superfamily.

name: Onica
classification_notes: Onica is built on a calligraphic structural base with asymmetrical slab serifs, sharp thick terminals and imperfect curves, placing it between a calligraphic serif and a slab serif with distinctive display characteristics.
summary: A calligraphic serif by Sanghyun Park that fuses asymmetrical slab serifs, sharp terminals and imperfect curves into a two-weight family.
description: Designed by Seoul-based Sanghyun Park, Onica is described as a chimera built on a calligraphic structural base, combining thick sharp terminals, asymmetrical slab serifs and intentionally imperfect curves. The contrast between these elements gives it a striking quality in display settings whilst retaining rhythm and texture at body sizes. The family is currently limited to Regular and Regular Italic, with 673 glyphs covering an extensive range of Latin-script languages.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Onica as a serif typeface conceived as a chimera, mixing diverse typographic genes on a calligraphic structural base. It combines thick sharp terminals, imperfect curves and asymmetrical slab serifs to form a hybrid that moves between strangeness and sensibility, delivering dynamic contrast in display settings and distinctive texture in body text.

name: Tocapu
classification_notes: A low-contrast geometric sans with angular counters, truncated cuts, and faceted details drawn from Andean geometric traditions. The tall x-height and architectural rhythm place it firmly in the geometric sans category.
summary: A geometric sans by Lima-based designer Paola Vecco, drawing on Andean geometric traditions to produce a clean face with truncated cuts and angular counters.
description: Built on an architectural logic, Tocapu uses faceted details, angular counters and a tall x-height to achieve stable typographic colour without sacrificing readability at text sizes. The family spans seven weights from ExtraLight to ExtraBold plus a variable font, and includes a stylistic ornaments set, alternates, oldstyle and tabular figures, ligatures and fractions. The cultural source material gives it a distinctive rhythm that separates it from generic geometric sans options in branding and editorial contexts.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Tocapu as a contemporary sans serif inspired by Andean geometry, built on an architectural logic with faceted details producing truncated cuts, angular counters and a precise, carved rhythm. With low contrast and a tall x-height it delivers comfortable reading in text sizes, and the family includes seven weights, a variable font and an extensive OpenType feature set including stylistic ornaments and alternates.

name: Tomato Grotesk
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a modern grotesk with geometric shapes and accentuated contrast; the high-contrast strokes and ink traps give it a display-leaning character uncommon in pure geometric grotesques.
summary: A modern geometric grotesque with pronounced stroke contrast and ink traps, available in nine weights with matching slanted cuts and a variable font.
description: Built around simple geometric shapes, Tomato Grotesk distinguishes itself through high contrast and tight spacing that holds up in small print and on screen. Ink traps reinforce its usability at small sizes, while at large dimensions the same features give it a lively, expressive quality suited to display work. The family spans 18 static styles plus a variable version, with an extensive OpenType feature set including stylistic alternates, ligatures, fractions and superiors, covering a broad Latin character set of over 800 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Tomato Grotesk as a modern grotesk family with simple geometric shapes and accentuated contrast that give it a strong display-like personality. They note that high contrast, tight spacing and ink traps make it well suited to both print and screen use at small sizes, while at large dimensions those same features produce a cheerful, versatile typeface. The family comprises nine weights and nine matching slanted versions, plus a variable version, with extended OpenType features and broad Latin language support.

name: Romek
classification_notes: Romek is a serif family drawing on early twentieth-century Antiqua sources including Genzsch-Antiqua, Orlando and Paganini, placing it within a transitional to old-style serif tradition with upright, text-capable forms.
summary: A serif family in 28 styles split between sharp and rounded variants, drawing on early twentieth-century Antiqua designs by Nebiolo and others.
description: Developed over three years, the family spans seven weights from Thin to Bold in both Sharp and Rounded versions, each with matching italics. Its sources include Genzsch-Antiqua (1906), Orlando (1920) and Paganini (1928), giving it a grounded, pre-modernist character suited to both display and text use. OpenType features run to small caps, old-style figures, ligatures, fractions and Roman numerals, with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Romek as a serif typeface family designed for a vast range of applications, refined over three years from a display typeface into a capable text font. They cite inspiration from Genzsch-Antiqua, Orlando and Paganini by Nebiolo, and note the family comprises 28 styles across sharp and rounded versions with an extensive OpenType feature set.

name: Wulkan
classification_notes: Wulkan is a serif family spanning Text, Headline and Display optical sizes, with high-contrast letterforms suited to editorial and expressive typesetting. Its range from Thin to Black with italics and variable font support places it firmly in the contemporary serif category.
summary: A three-optical-size serif family spanning 48 styles with a variable font axis covering weight and optical size.
description: Wulkan covers Text, Headline and Display optical sizes, each in eight weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, giving 48 static styles in total. Two variable fonts — upright and italic — expose continuous weight and optical-size axes for fine-grained control. OpenType features include oldstyle numerals, small caps, swash alternates for italics and three stylistic sets offering alternate forms for characters such as a, k, R and v.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry present Wulkan as a large serif family with Text, Headline and Display optical sizes, eight weights, italics, and variable font versions covering weight and optical size axes. OpenType features include ligatures, small caps, oldstyle numerals, swash characters, and multiple stylistic sets.

name: Brik
classification_notes: Brik is a display sans built around a deliberately brick-like geometric construction with hard angles, chunky corners and exaggerated ink traps, placing it firmly in the display category with a geometric, industrial character.
summary: A blunt geometric display family from BurnType with exaggerated ink traps, hard angles and chunky corners across five optical sizes and a condensed sub-family.
description: Designed by Michael Bagnardi of BurnType in Brooklyn and distributed via The Designers Foundry, Brik is built on a brick-like construction that favours hard angles and exaggerated ink traps over conventional refinement. Twenty static styles span five optical sizes from Regular to 4XL in both upright and oblique, with a matching condensed set and a variable font covering weight, slant and width axes. Revisited in 2020 and 2021, the family gained expanded glyph support, redrawn letters and new kerning, giving it more utility at text sizes than its display origins suggest.
foundry_blurb: BurnType Studio and The Designers Foundry describe Brik as a zero-bullshit type family with a blatant brick-like construction that is honest, coarse and unapologetic. Characteristic hard angles, chunky corners and exaggerated ink traps define its edge, balanced by subtle curves and strokes that add contrast to its rigid foundation. The family was thoroughly revisited in 2020 with expanded glyph support, completely new kerning and reconstructed letters, and is sold as individual styles or bundled as a variable font with a New York attitude.

name: Halisa
classification_notes: A neutral sans-serif with grotesque characteristics, available across five widths and six weights with italics, suggesting a utilitarian grotesque lineage designed for versatile typographic deployment.
summary: A neutral grotesque sans spanning five widths and six weights, available as variable fonts with weight, width and italic axes.
description: Sixty static styles cover Condensed through Extended widths, each running from Thin to Heavy with matching italics, giving the family broad reach across editorial, branding and display work. Two variable fonts, Halisa Condensed VF and Halisa VF, consolidate the range into weight, width and italic axes. Opentype alternates include a hexagon dot, single-storey a and g, and alternate German double s and ampersand.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry present Halisa as a large grotesque family with 60 styles across five widths and six weights, supported by variable font versions with weight, width and italic axes, and a set of OpenType alternates including single-storey letterforms and a hexagon dot feature.

name: Morion
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes Morion as a transitional serif typeface. Sharp details, high contrast and dynamic condensed italics confirm this classification, balancing text utility with display presence.
summary: A transitional serif by David Einwaller, originally sketched in 2014 and substantially revised for a 2020 re-release across six weights with matching italics.
description: Six years passed between the first sketches and the 2020 re-release, which brought an extended glyph set, revised spacing and four new weights to the original two. The design sits between text and display use: tight enough to run at small sizes in print, sharp enough to hold its own at large scale. Condensed, dynamic italics give it a contemporary edge that sits comfortably against the classical proportions of the roman cuts.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Morion as a balancing act between text and display type, with sharp details and dynamic condensed italics giving a contemporary, digital tone embedded within a classic transitional serif. The 2020 re-release revised and extended the entire glyph set with a focus on improved print performance at smaller sizes, and was accompanied by a 110-page paperback specimen. The typeface has been featured on It's Nice That, AIGA and Slanted, and has been used by clients including The Met and L'Oréal.

name: Serica
classification_notes: A uniwidth dot matrix family built on a 7-unit cap height grid, organised into three terminal-shape subfamilies (Circle, Square, Squircle). Its pixel-grid construction and embroidery origins place it firmly in display rather than general-purpose sans territory.
summary: A uniwidth dot matrix family in 23 styles, drawn from Dutch merklap embroidery and structured around three terminal shapes: Circle, Square and Squircle.
description: Built on a strict 7-unit cap height grid, the family avoids overlapping strokes to stay true to its embroidery reference, with steep diagonal angles producing a rhythmic texture across all weights. Three subfamilies spanning Thin to Black (Circle extending to Black) are supported by a variable font and a generous feature set including swashes, oldstyle numerals, compact and circular alternates, ligatures and localisation. Wide Latin Extended coverage runs to over 100 languages.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Serica as a uniwidth dot matrix type family built around three shape styles — Circle, Square and Squircle — spanning 23 styles plus a variable font, reinterpreting Dutch merklap embroidery through a contemporary lens. They note that the 7-unit cap height necessitates steep diagonal angles and produces a rhythmic texture, which can be modified via stylistic sets for circular or compact forms, and that the family offers an abundance of features including localisation, swashes, ligatures, alternative letters and number styles.

name: Berna
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a neo-grotesque, Berna draws on Swiss modernist tradition while incorporating more expressive curved forms and geometric details, placing it clearly within the neo-grotesque subcategory.
summary: A neo-grotesque with Swiss roots offered in 16 styles and a variable font file, with eleven stylistic sets providing alternates that shift the feel from geometric to more traditional.
description: Berna balances the structured geometry of Swiss modernism against softer, more expressive curves, distinguishing it from harder neo-grotesques. Sixteen static styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics, and a single variable font file covers both weight and italic axes. Eleven stylistic sets offer alternate forms for characters including the a, g, k, R and ampersand, broadening the range from clinical to character-driven settings.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Berna as a versatile neo-grotesque that merges modernist principles with Swiss tradition, reinterpreted through a contemporary and functional lens. The family balances geometric precision with more curved and expressive shapes, and includes extensive OpenType features and stylistic sets spanning from geometric sharpness to more traditional details. Vietnamese language support was added in May 2026.

name: Drool
classification_notes: Drool is a display serif with organic, sculptural forms inspired by H.R. Giger's biomechanical aesthetic, placing it firmly in the display category with a horror/dark arts subcategory.
summary: A biomechanical display serif inspired by H.R. Giger's Xenomorph design from Ridley Scott's 1979 film Alien, with curving terminals and connective-tissue-like strokes.
description: Two styles, Single and Double, share the same organic anatomy: terminals that curve as if grown, and a structural rhythm that reads as living tissue under tension. The letterforms suit large-scale applications where the uncanny quality reads clearly, particularly titles, posters and identity work with a dark or unsettling register. Discretionary ligatures and alternate characters for A, G, K, Q and R extend the expressive range.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Drool as inspired by H.R. Giger's Xenomorph design for Ridley Scott's Alien. They characterise it as a biomechanical serif where each character feels alive, as if grown rather than drawn, with curving terminals and slick connective tissue binding the anatomy together. They position it for titles, posters and identity systems that require an uncanny edge.

name: Cards
classification_notes: Cards is a contemporary grotesque sans-serif with a wide range of widths and weights. Its alternate letterforms and slanted cuts point to a humanist-grotesque sensibility suited to editorial and identity work.
summary: A large-scale grotesque family spanning six widths and eight weights, available as a variable font with width, weight and slant axes.
description: Running from Condensed to Wide across eight weights from Thin to Black, Cards offers 96 static styles plus slanted counterparts and a single variable font covering all three axes. OpenType features include small caps, old-style figures, ordinals, fractions and a generous set of alternate letterforms for characters such as a, g, r and t. The breadth of the family makes it well suited to publication design, branding systems and any context requiring consistent type across varied measures.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry present Cards as a versatile grotesque family with six width variants, eight weights, slanted styles and a variable font with width, weight and slant axes, supported by extensive OpenType features including alternates, small caps and multiple numeral styles.

name: Zoom Pro
classification_notes: Described as a 'brutal yet friendly display sans serif', Zoom Pro sits within the grotesque tradition but with deliberate calligraphic underpinnings and an expressive, display-oriented character.
summary: A display grotesque by Florian Paizs spanning six widths and six weights, with calligraphic details that come alive at large sizes.
description: Originally released as Zoom Grotesque between 2016 and 2019, this 36-style family runs from Narrow Hairline through to Brutal Bold, giving designers considerable range across width and weight. Subtle calligraphic characteristics distinguish it from more neutral grotesques, and an extensive OpenType feature set includes multiple alternate glyphs for letters such as R, Q, t and l. With 1,209 glyphs per weight and broad Latin Plus language support, it covers well over a hundred languages from Afrikaans to Zulu.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Zoom Pro as a brutal yet friendly display sans serif with six widths and six weights totalling 36 styles. They note its subtle calligraphic characteristics and strong personality at large sizes, and highlight its many alternate glyphs and OpenType features as making it highly adaptable and fun to use.

name: Fragen
classification_notes: The foundry describes Fragen as a text-display hybrid with a strong slab flavour, placing it squarely in the slab serif category. The spirited italics and mix of text and display utility suggest a contemporary humanist slab approach.
summary: A text-display hybrid slab serif by Lucas Descroix, offered in 16 styles across eight weights with spirited italics and a generous set of OpenType features.
description: Designed by French type and graphic designer Lucas Descroix, Fragen bridges text and display use through a slab construction that balances lively detail against typographic stability. The family's 16 styles span Thin to Black with matching italics, and the character set of 755 glyphs includes proportional oldstyle figures, small caps, discretionary ligatures, alternate letterforms and decorative patterns. Broad Latin language support covers over 80 languages, from Vietnamese to Zulu.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Fragen as a text-display hybrid with a strong slab flavour and spirited italics, available in 16 styles. They note it balances diverse and sometimes contradictory elements into one cohesive typeface that is stable but lively, with a generous set of symbols, patterns, ligatures and alternates.

name: Goodman
classification_notes: A revival of Gesh Ortega Roman 275 by Gerhard Schwekendiek, with bracketed serifs and rhythm built on interconnected stem serifs forming an elliptical motif at joins — consistent with transitional serif construction.
summary: A revival of Gerhard Schwekendiek's Gesh Ortega Roman 275, reworked by Ethan Nakache across six weights with matching italics and variable font files.
description: Designed by Ethan Nakache over four years, it preserves the rhythm and character of the original Gesh Ortega Roman while refining the italics and adding contemporary versatility for modern typesetting. A defining structural detail is the interconnected serifs at stem joins, which form a consistent elliptical curve across characters. Available in 12 static styles plus two variable fonts covering the full weight range, with OpenType features including oldstyle numerals, tabular figures and alternate letterforms.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Goodman as a vibrant revival of the iconic Gesh Ortega Roman 275 by Gerhard Schwekendiek, designed by Ethan Nakache over four-plus years. They emphasise its commitment to preserving the original design's essence while adding contemporary versatility, with particular attention to the interconnected serif stems that create a distinctive elliptical rhythm across the typeface.

name: Gatch
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif in the tradition of Futura and Neuzeit Grotesk, distinguished by tension between full-round and narrow glyph proportions and a uni-width system across nine weights.
summary: A geometric sans by David Einwaller that pairs Futura-derived principles with a uni-width system and variable uppercase width axes across nine weights.
description: Designed between 2017 and 2023, Gatch draws on Futura and Neuzeit Grotesk while introducing deliberate tension between circular and narrow glyph proportions, taking cues from Joseph Churchward's Crossbred. Its uni-width system keeps letter spacing consistent across the weight range, making it practical for UI and display work where layout stability matters. A variable font adds three axes controlling the width of straights, rounds and diagonals in uppercase letters independently, alongside a broad set of stylistic alternates.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Gatch as bringing playfulness and generous forms to geometric sans-serifs, inspired by Churchward's Crossbred and grounded in the functional principles of Futura and Neuzeit Grotesk. The uni-width system enables weight changes without disrupting text flow, making it suited to sensitive displays, UI design and printed media. Three variable axes allow independent control of uppercase glyph geometry, offering an near-infinite range of headline combinations across nine static weights and a variable font.

name: Bisel
classification_notes: A single-weight display typeface available in four widths from Regular to XXCondensed, drawing on classic condensed display types in the tradition of Lubalin and Haettenschweiler. Its mechanical character and headline-focused design place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-weight display family in four widths, drawing on condensed European and American type traditions associated with Herb Lubalin and Walter Haettenschweiler.
description: Designed by Fase Studio between 2022 and 2023, it blends features from several classic condensed typefaces into something with a predominantly mechanical character, offset by subtle irregularities that keep it from feeling cold. The family runs from Black to Black XXCondensed and is also available as a variable font with a width axis, supported by standard ligatures, case-sensitive forms and a squared stylistic set. Extended Latin coverage makes it suitable for a broad range of European languages.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Bisel as a display typeface inspired by classic European and American condensed types and designers like Herb Lubalin, Tom Carnase and Walter Haettenschweiler. It remixes features from several classics to produce a mechanical appearance offset by subtle humanist gestures, and is designed for loud, unmistakable headlines. Available in one weight and four widths, including as a variable font, with an extended Latin character set.

name: Dysto
classification_notes: Dysto is a display-oriented geometric sans with monospaced architecture, modular construction and reverse contrast strokes drawn from 1980s sci-fi visual culture. Its highly stylised letterforms and variable axes push it firmly into display territory.
summary: A variable display sans with monospaced construction and reverse contrast, drawing on 1980s science fiction visual language across six widths and three weights.
description: Built on modular, directly proportionate forms, Dysto spans 36 static styles plus a variable font with weight, width and slant axes. Six stylistic sets handle serifs, alternate OQ8, X variants and a modified D, while 58 discretionary ligatures support expressive typesetting. The 22.5-degree slant and wide width range make it well suited to poster work, motion graphics and identity projects that need a retro-futurist register.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Dysto as a fusion of futuristic dreams and retro design, crafted from the visual language of 1980s sci-fi films. They highlight its monospaced architecture, modular construction and reverse contrast strokes alongside variable and OpenType capabilities including six stylistic sets and extensive ligatures. The family is pitched for real-world use in unreal worlds, from holographic advertising and helmet displays to hoverboard stickers and neon signage on Third Millennium satellite hotels.

name: Granda
classification_notes: Explicitly rooted in the Grotesk genre with lively proportions and quirky details drawn from early grotesque models, distinguishing it from the sterile neutrality of later Swiss grotesques.
summary: A 27-style grotesque with lively proportions and an unusual reclined axis, packaged in a single variable font.
description: Drawn from early grotesque models rather than the cleaned-up Swiss tradition, Granda brings visible quirks and warmth to a genre often associated with sterility. The family spans nine weights from Hair to Heavy, each available in upright, italic and a distinct reclined variant that tilts in the opposite direction to the italic. All 27 styles are accessible via a variable font with weight and slant axes, and OpenType features include proportional oldstyle and tabular lining figures.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Granda as a sans-serif rooted in the Grotesk genre, with lively proportions that bring warmth to text. The italic's contrasted structure adds emphasis and personality, while Granda Reclined offers a playful counterbalance, opening up hierarchy and headline options as an extra display style within the system. Inspired by the quirks and unexpected treatments found in early grotesque examples, the foundry liken it to a block of flats rather than a glass skyscraper, revealing individual personality through variety.

name: Rockbox
classification_notes: Rockbox is a display typeface with dynamic, industrial characteristics; its bold, geometric construction and multiple slant variants place it firmly in the display category, designed for expressive poster and editorial use.
summary: A single-weight display face by Kara Zichittella, originally drawn for an experimental music release, offered in Regular, Slant and Backslant cuts.
description: Designed by Berlin-based Zeitype for a poster promoting Dane Close's 'MIC' LP on Power Station Records, Rockbox carries the industrial and dynamic qualities of its musical source material. The family offers three stylistic variants — Regular, Slant and Backslant — alongside a wide set of alternates accessible via upper and lowercase keys. Suited to record covers, posters and editorial work where an angular, experimental tone is needed.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Rockbox as a display typeface designed by Zeitype, originally created for a poster promoting the 'MIC' LP release by Dane Close on Power Station Records. They note its dynamic and industrial characteristics as reflections of the LP's experimental sound, and highlight the range of alternates available across its three styles: Regular, Slant and Backslant.

name: BPM
classification_notes: BPM features a subtle reverse contrast construction with swooping, rhythmic letterforms inspired by audio engineering interfaces, placing it firmly in the display category with a distinctive decorative character.
summary: A reverse-contrast display typeface with swooping, rhythm-driven letterforms inspired by audio engineering brands and interfaces.
description: Built around a subtle reverse contrast and melodic stroke construction, letters rise and dip according to their individual forms, producing a visual cadence across longer settings. Three static weights from Light to Bold are accompanied by a variable font for stepless weight control, and an extensive set of alternates for M, Q, W, R, A, E, F and other characters makes it particularly useful for logo and wordmark work. Language support spans well over a hundred Latin-based scripts.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe BPM (Beats Per Minute) as an extended typeface with a subtle reverse contrast, inspired by digital and analogue audio engineering brands and interfaces. They note that its melodic roots show in the swooping construction of each character, which extends, dips and rises to create rhythmic harmony in use. The family comes in three weights plus a variable version and includes a large variety of alternates suited to logo and wordmark design.

name: Reptil
designer: Raphaël de la Morinerie & Ethan Nakache
classification_notes: Built on a circular geometric foundation with compact capitals and distinctive lowercase forms, Reptil is a contemporary revival of the 1971 Letraset typeface Turtle, placing it firmly in the display category with a retro-geometric subcategory.
summary: A contemporary revival of Bob Newman's 1971 Letraset typeface Turtle, rebuilt on circular geometry by Raphaël de la Morinerie and Ethan Nakache.
description: Designed by Raphaël de la Morinerie and Ethan Nakache, Reptil takes the circular structure of the long-forgotten Letraset Turtle and reworks it with a contemporary edge, pushing legibility to its limits through compact capitals and arresting lowercase forms. The result sits somewhere between retro novelty and functional display type, with OpenType alternates offering one-shape variants of r and t alongside three shapes of m. Coverage extends to 344 glyphs across a wide range of Latin-based languages.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Reptil as a contemporary interpretation of the Turtle typeface, originally designed by Bob Newman and published by Letraset in 1971. Turtle fell into obscurity before being digitised in the 2000s, and Reptil revives its circular foundation while combining vintage style with a contemporary edge, exploring the limits of legibility through compact capitals and distinctive lowercase forms.

name: Super
classification_notes: An all-capitals display typeface with ultra-sleek, razor-edged construction explicitly referencing 1970s and 80s sci-fi and futurist aesthetics, placing it firmly in retro display territory.
summary: An all-capitals display pair from BurnType's Michael Bagnardi, channelling 1970s and 80s science fiction aesthetics through ultra-sharp, sleek letterforms.
description: Super comes in two styles, Eighty and Seventy, both built on a single all-capitals frame with razor-sharp details that reference the era of Sorayama, Frazetta and the NASA worm logotype. Discretionary ligatures for common pairings such as es, mm, rs and ks add typographic texture within the limited glyph set of 218 characters. Latin language support spans a broad range of African and European languages, making it more versatile than its narrow display brief might suggest.
foundry_blurb: BurnType describe Super as a futuristic love letter to sex and science fiction in the 1970s and 80s, citing Sorayama, Frazetta, Barbarella, Blade Runner and the NASA worm as touchstones. The two high-performance display faces are built on a single all-capitals frame with ultra-sleek construction and razor-sharp details, described in characteristically direct BurnType fashion as big, loud and unapologetically zipper-melting.

name: Genevoix
classification_notes: Built on simple geometric forms with explicit reference to Bauhaus aesthetics, placing it squarely in the geometric sans tradition. The stencil and extended display variants add a mechanical, constructed quality consistent with that lineage.
summary: A geometric sans in twelve styles with Bauhaus references, split across Text and Display subfamilies, with stencil and extended cuts throughout the display range.
description: Designed by Roman Seban and built on simple geometric forms, Genevoix draws on Bauhaus aesthetics while introducing alternate letterforms to introduce rhythm and warmth. The Text subfamily covers Light, Regular, Italic and Bold for body use, while the Display subfamily extends into Regular, Bold, Extended Regular and Extended Bold, each also available as a stencil cut. Variable Font support runs across three axes in the display styles. The broad Latin coverage spans over eighty languages.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Genevoix as a geometric typeface family paying tribute to Bauhaus aesthetics, with a mechanical yet warm appearance suited to print, visual identities, signage and websites. The family divides into Text and Display subfamilies, with Display offering eight styles including stencil and extended variants, all available as Variable Fonts. Each style includes alternate letterforms to enhance visual rhythm in compositions.

name: Averta
classification_notes: Averta is a geometric sans-serif with a clean, circular construction. The PE (Pan-European) designation and breadth of stylistic sets for Latin, Cyrillic, Greek and Bulgarian scripts confirm its broad humanist-geometric heritage.
summary: A pan-European geometric sans available in nine weights across three widths, with full variable font support and extensive Cyrillic, Greek and Bulgarian coverage.
description: Spanning Condensed, Narrow and standard widths, each in nine weights with matching italics, Averta PE totals 54 styles plus variable font versions. OpenType features include oldstyle numerals, case-sensitive forms and eleven stylistic sets addressing alternate letterforms for Latin, Greek, Cyrillic, Bulgarian and Siberian scripts. The result is a workhorse geometric sans suited to UI, editorial and identity work across multilingual environments.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry present Averta PE as a geometric sans-serif family with broad script support, offering Condensed, Narrow and standard width subfamilies each spanning nine weights from Extrathin to Black, all available as variable fonts with a comprehensive set of OpenType features including multiple Cyrillic, Greek and Bulgarian stylistic alternates.

name: Shape
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes Shape as a contemporary geometric type family built with precision and simplicity, consistent with the geometric sans-serif tradition of circular, constructed letterforms.
summary: A contemporary geometric sans in 18 styles by Christoph York, spanning Hairline to Super with matching italics throughout.
description: Designed by Berlin and London-based Christoph York, Shape is a geometric sans built around an open, content-first aesthetic with a subtle warmth to its construction. Nine weights from Hairline to Super are paired with corresponding italics, giving the family broad range for both display and text applications. OpenType features include discretionary ligatures, case-sensitive forms, tabular figures and alternate characters for g and y.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Shape as a contemporary geometric type family in 18 styles, built with precision, simplicity and subtle warmth. Flexibility is the founding principle, with an open and accessible aesthetic designed to put content first and convey information with clarity and purity.

name: Grandmaster
classification_notes: Extremely condensed vertical structure with near-kinetic rhythm, inspired by 20th-century Antique Press fonts and designed primarily for titling and editorial use rather than continuous text.
summary: A very condensed titling typeface by Lucas Descroix, drawn from 20th-century Antique Press sources and named in tribute to Grandmaster Flash.
description: Designed by French type designer Lucas Descroix, Grandmaster takes inspiration from 20th-century Antique Press fonts, using an extremely vertical structure and highly regular counter-shapes to create a near-kinetic quality on the page. Five weights from Thin to Black each come with matching italics, and the family is also available as a variable font spanning the full weight range. OpenType features include oldstyle numerals, discretionary ligatures, alternate lowercase a and g forms, and hairline punctuation.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Grandmaster as a very condensed typeface inspired by 20th-century Antique Press fonts, exploring the border between text and abstraction. They note its extremely vertical structure and regular counter-shapes make it an almost-kinetic titling typeface suited to incisive editorial layouts, and say the name pays tribute to Grandmaster Flash, pioneer of hip-hop DJing, cutting, scratching and mixing.

name: Habanero
classification_notes: The foundry describes it as an experimental display typeface combining art nouveau and calligraphic influences with playful unpredictability, placing it firmly in the decorative display category.
summary: A free variable display typeface by Jude Grover, blending art nouveau and calligraphic forms across five weights with OpenType alternates.
description: Designed by Jude Grover while based in London, Habanero draws on art nouveau and calligraphic influences to produce a display face with an unpredictable, expressive quality. Five weights run from Thin to Black, all available as a variable font, with OpenType features including standard and discretionary ligatures and a stylistic set affecting A, L, I, R and M. Broad Latin language support covers over a hundred languages, and the full family is offered free to download.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Habanero as a spirited experimental display typeface combining art nouveau and calligraphic influences with playful unpredictability. Bold and dynamic, it ranges from sweet to spicy across five weights with a variable option and a small set of OpenType features including ligatures and stylistic alternatives. It is their first free font.

name: Acai
classification_notes: A blocky, geometric display typeface inspired by street graffiti and brutalist architecture, with no italic cuts and a strong width-axis variable axis. The design is purely headline-oriented with no text-setting intent.
summary: A geometric display typeface sketched in Rio de Janeiro, drawing from street graffiti and brutalist architecture across six width variants from Ultra Condensed to Ultra Expanded.
description: Designed by Mateusz Machalski with roots in a 2017 visit to Rio de Janeiro, Acai pulls from bold street lettering and the collision of brutalist and colonial architecture. Six width styles run from Ultra Condensed to Ultra Expanded, with a variable font version added in March 2024 alongside additional weights. Two stylistic sets offer alternate single-storey forms for 'a' and 'g', and the family now covers Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Acai as a modern display typeface originally sketched during a visit to Rio de Janeiro, inspired by geometric street graffiti, greasy headline typefaces and the architectural mix of brutalism with colonial buildings. Suited to display and headline settings, its blocky design is intended to work with colour. The design dates to 2017, with further refinement over the years and a variable version with additional weights added in March 2024, plus Greek and Cyrillic support.

name: Averta Standard
classification_notes: Averta is explicitly described as a geometric sans serif, drawing on early European grotesques and American gothics, with purely geometric rounds, open apertures and low contrast strokes.
summary: A geometric sans by Kostas Bartsokas that blends European grotesque and American gothic influences across nine weights with true italics and support for over 400 languages.
description: Drawn with purely geometric rounds, open apertures and low contrast strokes, it aims for a modernist, neutral and friendly character suited to text and display use alike. The family spans Extrathin to Black with matching true italics, and extends into narrow and condensed widths for tight headlines. Cyrillic support earned third place at the 2017 Granshan Awards, and the family is also available as a variable font covering both weight and width axes.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Averta as a geometric sans serif that brings together features from early European grotesques and American gothics. Designed by Kostas Bartsokas, the name comes from the Greek word meaning to act or speak openly and without moderation. The typeface includes small caps, multiple figure sets, localised letterforms for Bulgarian, Macedonian, Russian and Serbian, and covers more than 400 languages across Latin, Greek, Cyrillic and Vietnamese scripts.

name: Bacalar
classification_notes: Described as brutalist and mechanical with geometric forms, very thin details and reversed contrast, drawing from vernacular bus signage typography. Its extreme width range and display character place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A geometric display type family drawn from vernacular bus signage in Mexico's Yucatan peninsula, spanning ten width variants from Turbo Condensed to Turbo Expanded.
description: Designed by Mateusz Machalski and inspired by the lettering found on colectivo buses in the Yucatan peninsula, the family pairs geometric construction with thin details and reversed contrast for a mechanical, brutalist character. Ten static width styles plus a variable font cover an unusually broad range, from tightly compressed to aggressively expanded. Updated to Pro in November 2025, it now includes Cyrillic and Greek alongside an extensive Latin language roster.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Bacalar as inspired by vernacular typography on colectivo buses in the Yucatan peninsula, Mexico. Geometric forms contrast with very thin details and reversed contrast, giving it a brutalist, mechanical character delivered across 10 width styles plus a variable font. The Pro update in November 2025 added Cyrillic and Greek support.

name: Slimak
classification_notes: Slimak is explicitly described as a fatface type family, a genre originating in early 19th-century Britain characterised by extreme stroke contrast and bold, attention-grabbing weight. The high-contrast letterforms with strong terminals confirm this classification.
summary: A three-optical-size fatface with rounded corners, calligraphic details and a high x-height, designed for high-impact display work.
description: Slimak draws on the early 19th-century British fatface tradition, adapting it with rounded corners and calligraphic details that soften the genre's characteristic drama. Three optical sizes - Small, Medium and Large - allow the family to hold up across different scales, supported by a high x-height that aids legibility at smaller settings. The package includes icons, dingbats and ornaments alongside OpenType features such as circled numbers and alternate letterforms for a, g and y.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Slimak as a fatface type family with strong terminals and rounded corners, designed in three optical sizes for readability across contexts. They note its calligraphic details and high x-height, and highlight the inclusion of icons, dingbats and ornaments as tools for creating cohesive visual compositions in branding, editorial and digital interface work.

name: Afronaut
classification_notes: A display sans with a distinctive character set drawing on vernacular African lettering, Arabic script influence and space-age futurist aesthetics, placing it firmly outside standard text classification and into expressive display territory.
summary: A seven-weight display sans by Mateusz Machalski, drawing on West African vernacular lettering and futurist space-travel aesthetics for its distinctive letterforms.
description: Inspired by Bartek Sabela's book on Zambia's space programme, this typeface blends vernacular lettering Machalski encountered in Guinea-Bissau, Gambia, Senegal and Mauritania with a futuristic, space-travel sensibility. Seven weights from Thin to Bold are complemented by a variable font, and many letters offer three alternate forms accessible via OpenType stylistic sets. Language support extends to Latin, Greek and Cyrillic, covering a broad range of African and European scripts.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Afronaut as a typeface born from a brief set after reading about the Zambian conquest of space, designed to look like a mix of vernacular Arabic scripts, futuristic space-travel typography and unique lettering found during travels through West Africa. It includes 7 weights, a variable font, multiple stylistic sets per letter, and supports Greek and Cyrillic in addition to an extensive Latin language list.

name: Random
classification_notes: Random is a bold display typeface built around deliberate inconsistency, using six alternating glyph variants per character to produce contextual variation. Its extreme letterforms and expressive weight place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-weight display typeface that uses six glyph alternates per character to produce automatic contextual variation and a curated sense of disorder.
description: Originally released in 2014 and brought back from the vault, Random builds its personality through inconsistency rather than uniformity. Each glyph has six alternates split across lowercase and uppercase, which shuffle automatically to prevent repetition and give set text an organic, hand-crafted feel. The result is a bold display face that reads as effortlessly varied while remaining visually coherent across a wide range of Latin-based languages.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Random as an eye-catching expressive typeface originally released in 2014, featuring six glyph alternates per character that shuffle automatically to create random contextual combinations, exploring the visual value of letters and extremes in form. They note that inconsistency is its defining quality, with small details and an overall bold attitude producing what they call seemingly effortless yet well-curated randomness.

name: Yalla
classification_notes: A single-weight display typeface drawing on geometric Arabic headline letterforms, with strong angular geometry offset by calligraphic details; fits the display category given its expressive, single-style nature.
summary: A single-weight geometric display face by Mateusz Machalski, shaped by the strong Arabic headline typography and calligraphic contrasts encountered in Cairo.
description: Designed after Machalski's trip to Cairo, Yalla draws on the geometric forms and calligraphic details characteristic of Arabic headline type, translating those contrasts into a Latin display face. With only a Bold weight and 353 glyphs, it is a deliberately narrow tool suited to headlines, posters and branding where a strong, singular voice is needed. Language support spans a broad range of Latin-based scripts including European, African and Southeast Asian languages.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Yalla as a typeface inspired by Mateusz Machalski's trip to Cairo, where the geometric forms and calligraphic details of Arabic headline type fed the design. It is available in a single Bold style with broad Latin language support covering over 80 languages.

name: Hardy
classification_notes: Hardy is a display typeface with a geometric construction defined by circular cuts at every angular intersection, replacing 45° and 90° angles with circles. Its strong personality and chiselled form place it firmly in the geometric display category.
summary: A single-weight geometric display face by Wade Jeffree that replaces every angular intersection with a circle sized to the angle it softens.
description: Designed by New York-based Wade Jeffree, Hardy applies a consistent rule across its construction: wherever two strokes meet at 45° or 90°, a circle replaces the corner, with the circle's diameter determined by the angle's measure. The result is a face that reads as both chiselled and softened, giving it a slightly unusual personality within the geometric display genre. Available as a single Regular weight, it suits headline and branding work where a distinctive geometric character is wanted without resorting to conventional sharp-cornered letterforms.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Hardy as a display typeface where circular cuts replace every angled intersection. For each 45° and 90° angle, a circle softens the edge while maintaining a chiselled appearance, with each circle's size determined by the measure of the angle. They characterise the result as a largely masculine yet somewhat awkward geometric face with strong personality.

name: Pyxis
classification_notes: Pyxis is an experimental display typeface built from deconstructed, sharp geometric letterforms that push legibility to its limits, placing it firmly in the display/experimental category.
summary: A three-weight experimental display face by Melbourne-based Studio io, built from deconstructed letterforms using sharp geometric forms and repetition.
description: Originating as a typographic poster project, Pyxis grew from a loose set of deconstructed letters into a full character set across Light, Regular and Heavy weights. Each character is constructed through geometric repetition, creating forms that deliberately test legibility while retaining a sense of play. The result suits high-impact editorial and poster work where visual oddity is an asset.
foundry_blurb: Pyxis is described by the foundry as an experimental display typeface designed by Studio io, born from an exploration of deconstructed letterforms for typographic posters. Using sharp geometric forms and repetition, it pushes the limits of legibility while maintaining play, irreverence and oddness. It supports an extensive range of Latin-based languages with a glyph count of 369.

name: Segma
classification_notes: Segma is built on circular geometry with low-contrast strokes and open apertures, placing it squarely in the geometric sans tradition, though the foundry notes it retains contemporary utility rather than strict historical revival.
summary: A geometric sans-serif in eight weights with matching italics, drawn by Christoph York with circular forms and open apertures for comfortable reading across branding and text use.
description: Built on circular geometry with low-contrast strokes, Segma sits in the broader geometric sans tradition while leaning towards contemporary utility rather than strict revival. Open apertures keep it readable at text sizes, and the full 16-style family, spanning Thin to Black with matching italics, gives it decent range for identity and editorial work. Stylistic alternates for 'a', 'j', 'y' and 't' allow some personality adjustment, and OpenType support covers tabular figures, case-sensitive forms and a slashed zero.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Segma as a geometric sans-serif type family of eight weights plus matching italics, with circular geometry informing a contemporary modern aesthetic. Open apertures and low-contrast strokes are noted as contributing to comfortable reading, and the family is positioned as building upon geometric traditions while remaining firmly in the present.

name: Goliagolia
classification_notes: A display typeface blending baroque letter forms with geometric, hand-drawn imperfection. The contrast between irregular stroke construction and sharp modern curves places it firmly in the display category with a decorative serif subcategory.
summary: An Italian-designed display face that mixes baroque letter forms with deliberately imperfect hand-drawn geometry, producing an unusually spirited contrast between rough construction and sharp curves.
description: Designed by Alex Valentina, Goliagolia blends baroque calligraphic traits with geometrically imperfect strokes, each letter carrying its own distinct details while remaining coherent within the family. Two styles are offered: Regular and Sharp, the latter pushing the modern display curves further. The typeface has attracted attention in editorial and identity contexts where an eccentric, high-contrast serif voice is needed, earning a description from AIGA Design as 'a spaceship collided with a baroque cathedral'.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Goliagolia as born from a desire to merge a modern look with classic baroque traits. Each letter was designed with distinct details that still belong to the overall style, and the typeface was drawn with a handwriting sensibility to remain geometrically imperfect, allowing inexact hand lines to meet modern sharp display curves and create an uncommon spirit and contrast.

name: Fran
classification_notes: Fran is a slab serif with unbracketed serifs and subtle stroke contrast that intensifies across the weight range, fitting the Egyptian slab tradition.
summary: A six-weight Egyptian slab serif by Max Saille, with unbracketed serifs and contrast that builds from delicate in the lighter cuts to chunky and assertive at the heavier end.
description: Designed by Dutch-born, New York-based Max Saille, the family spans Thin to Ultra across six weights, with the lighter styles suited to continuous text and the heavier cuts intended for display use. The unbracketed serifs become progressively chunkier as weight increases, giving the family a coherent but varied character across settings. OpenType features include oldstyle and lining numerals, case-sensitive forms, contextual alternates and an alternate set of accented uppercase characters for tighter titling.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Fran as a strong yet elegant slab serif available in six weights, from a gentle thin to a vigorous ultra. They note its subtle, quiet character at lighter weights that grows louder as weight increases, with the delicate contrast becoming more profound and the serifs chunkier and energetic at heavier settings, making lighter styles suited to continuous text and heavier ones to attention-demanding display sizes.

name: Skanaus
classification_notes: Skanaus blends classical and contemporary genre traits with expressive, unconventional details that push it toward display territory; its described 'romance and movement' across the glyph set aligns with a decorative or lyrical serif sensibility.
summary: A single-weight serif by Daniel Gremme that draws on Lithuanian culture and architecture to produce letterforms sitting between classical convention and contemporary invention.
description: Begun during an exchange semester in Vilnius and supervised by Prof. Aušra Lisauskiene and Henning Skibbe, Skanaus exists in two optical styles: Regular and Display. The Display cut adds a set of alternates, including variant g and ß forms, alongside ligatures and fraction support. Its wild, movement-driven details make it most suited to editorial headlines, cultural identities and poster work.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Skanaus as mingling between type genres, with wild details creating unexpected forms that bring a sense of romance and movement across the glyph set. They note the design was begun in Vilnius during an exchange semester, with elements influenced by the culture and architecture of the region, and that it represents Daniel Gremme's first type release.

name: Rialto Script
classification_notes: A monolinear swashed display script with a slanted axis, drawn from dynamic handwriting and inspired by Polish neon signage. Sits firmly in the display script category due to its heavy use of alternates, swashes and contextual substitution.
summary: A monolinear display script by Zuzanna Rogatty, drawn from Polish communist-era neon signs and built with extensive alternates and swashes for varied typesetting.
description: Designed by Warsaw-based Zuzanna Rogatty, the typeface takes its cues from the idiosyncratic neon signage of communist-era Poland. Glyphs sit slightly above the baseline on a slanted axis, and the family offers contextual alternates, standard and discretionary ligatures, initials, and capital and lowercase swashes including beginning, ending and dash variants. A set of block letters is accessible via the small caps feature, making it suited to titles, logos and sign-style work.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Rialto Script as a display script inspired by old neon signs from communist-era Poland, noting that its large number of alternates and swashes make every word unique, just as those neon signs were. The font is monolinear, swashed and drawn from dynamic, rhythmical handwriting, with glyphs sitting slightly above the baseline on a slanted axis.

name: Optimum Compress
classification_notes: The foundry describes it as a futuristic blackletter built from a limited set of geometric base shapes, while retaining references to hand-drawn Textura type of the 13th and 14th century, placing it firmly in a geometric interpretation of the blackletter tradition.
summary: A single-weight geometric blackletter drawing on 13th and 14th-century Textura traditions, built from a reduced set of constructed forms.
description: Designed by Simon of Melbourne-based Studio io, Optimum Compress reduces Textura blackletter to a minimal vocabulary of geometric shapes, giving it a sharp, contemporary edge without entirely abandoning its calligraphic roots. It is available in a single Regular weight with a glyph count of 325, covering an extensive range of Latin-based languages including most European and African scripts. Best suited to display and editorial use where a structured, futuristic take on blackletter is called for.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Optimum Compress as a futuristic blackletter constructed from a limited number of base shapes and forms. They note it has a strong geometric edge while retaining subtle authentic references to hand-drawn Textura type of the 13th and 14th century. It was designed by Simon of Studio io, a Melbourne-based designer with over eleven years of practice spanning illustration and art direction.

name: Pivot
classification_notes: Pivot is a display typeface built from circular forms and cuts inspired by LCD digital watch digits. Its strict constructivist rules and retro-futurist aesthetic place it firmly in the geometric display category.
summary: A geometric display typeface drawn from the constrained character forms of LCD digital watches, delivered in Regular and Deco variants.
description: Designed by Australian illustrator Karan Singh, Pivot takes the rigid, circular logic of LCD watch digits and applies it as a strict system for constructing every glyph. The result sits in a retro-futurist register, with both a standard Regular cut and an ornamental Deco variant extending its range into more decorative territory. Coverage spans Latin Extended with 630 glyphs across the two styles.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Pivot as futuristic yet retro, inspired by the digit forms on digital watches and the constraints of LCD technology. Strict rules govern each glyph, built using essentially only circular forms and cuts. It is delivered in two styles, Regular and Deco, designed by Karan Singh, an Australian artist and illustrator based in Tokyo.

name: Rasmus
classification_notes: Loosely derived from classic oldstyle serifs such as Times and Sabon, but with experimental details including oblique serifs and a larger x-height in the Bold weight, placing it in a contemporary serif category rather than a strict historical revival.
summary: A four-style contemporary serif by Markus John, drawing loosely from Times and Sabon while adding oblique serifs and a notably larger x-height in the Bold cut.
description: Designed in 2014 by German designer Markus John, the family runs to Regular, Bold and their matching italics, with 381 glyphs per style and broad Latin language support covering over 70 languages. The Bold weight carries a larger x-height than the Regular, giving it a distinct visual weight beyond conventional bold compensation. Oblique serifs lend the face an angular quality that separates it from its classical references. In use across editorial, branding and culture-adjacent projects.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Rasmus as a contemporary serif designed by Markus John in 2014, loosely inspired by classic serifs like Times and Sabon but featuring experimental details. They highlight two distinguishing characteristics: oblique serifs throughout, and a larger x-height in the Bold weight for added legibility and presence.

name: Elegant Lux Pro
classification_notes: A slim, technologically constructed sans serif inspired by Elegant Grotesk, a 1928 geometric grotesque. Round corners soften its geometric construction, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition with a warmer character.
summary: A single-weight slim geometric sans with over 3,700 glyphs, extensive ligatures and multiple stylistic sets, rooted in Hans Möhring's 1928 Elegant Grotesk.
description: Designed by Florian Paizs of Schoener Studio, this slim sans takes Möhring's Elegant Grotesk as its starting point and rebuilds it with a geometric rigour softened by rounded corners. The OpenType font ships with 100-plus letter-combination ligatures, five stylistic sets covering both upper and lowercase alternates, small caps, ornaments and fractions. Language support stretches across Central, Western and Eastern European scripts including Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Elegant Lux Pro as a slim sans serif designed by Schoener in 2015, inspired by Elegant Grotesk, originally drawn by Hans Möhring for Schriftgiesserei D Stempel AG in 1928. They note its technological construction is warmed by round corners that reference the appearance of metal type printing, and highlight the font's 3,739 glyphs, extensive ligatures and alternates for a modern, experimental approach.

name: Luxus Gothic
classification_notes: A modern interpretation of Fette Fraktur, the 19th-century German blackletter style. The page describes it as a refined gothic typeface drawn from that tradition, making fraktur the precise subcategory.
summary: A single-weight modernisation of Fette Fraktur, drawn by Drew Melton between 2013 and 2015 with slightly tighter proportions and finer detailing than the 19th-century original.
description: Drew Melton drew Luxus Gothic over two years as a contemporary reworking of Johann Christian Bauer's Fette Fraktur of 1850, a typeface that became a standard across German foundries in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The design is slightly more condensed than its source material, with refined details intended to suit modern applications in branding and display. Language support extends across a wide range of Latin-script languages including German, French, Spanish and numerous African languages.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Luxus Gothic as a refined gothic typeface designed to be a modern interpretation of Fette Fraktur, originally created by Johann Christian Bauer in 1850. They note that it is slightly more condensed than its reference with finer details suited to contemporary use, and that the design was completed between 2013 and 2015 by Drew Melton.

name: Function
classification_notes: Caps-only display typeface built on a grid and informed by constructivist and utilitarian letterform traditions, with stencil-influenced alternates accessed via OpenType.
summary: A caps-only constructivist display typeface by graphic artist Anthony Burrill, built on a grid with stencil-influenced alternate characters.
description: Designed by Anthony Burrill as a response to collected examples of utilitarian and grid-based lettering, it offers two widths, Regular and Condensed, with stencil and extended variants planned. Alternate characters are accessible via upper/lowercase key switching and OpenType features, giving the typeface some compositional flexibility despite its strict geometric construction. Its roots in persuasive print and constructivist graphic traditions suit it to posters, campaigns and headline work.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Function as a caps-only display typeface designed by Anthony Burrill, informed by utilitarian and grid-based type. The constructivist-influenced design comes in Regular and Condensed styles, with alternate characters available under upper/lowercase keys and via OpenType features.

name: Atiza Text
classification_notes: A contemporary serif with tall x-height, low contrast and strong asymmetrical serifs, designed explicitly for editorial and long-text legibility at small sizes.
summary: A compact three-style serif by Pablo Bosch, built around a tall x-height and asymmetrical serifs for reliable legibility at small text sizes.
description: Designed by Pablo Bosch and published through The Designers Foundry, Atiza Text is an editorial serif optimised for long-form reading. Low stroke contrast and strong asymmetrical serifs keep the type stable at small sizes, while brush-stroke-like terminals become apparent at display scales. The family covers three styles, Regular, Italic and Bold, with 816 glyphs and broad OpenType support including small caps, fractions, tabular figures and multiple ampersand alternates across 200-plus Latin languages.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Atiza as a contemporary serif designed for editorial use, with a tall x-height, relatively low contrast and strong asymmetrical serifs that make it extremely legible at very small sizes. At larger sizes, brush-stroke-like terminal details become visible. The three-style system supports over 200 Latin languages and includes a wide range of OpenType features.

name: GabyGaby
classification_notes: An extreme experimental typeface with erratic, fluid forms and dual letterform variants per character; firmly in the display category with no conventional structural classification applying.
summary: An experimental display typeface by Alex Valentina with two distinct letterform variants per character, giving the family an erratic, kinetic quality.
description: Designed by Italian graphic designer Alex Valentina, GabyGaby offers two separate styles for each letter mapped to upper and lowercase keys, allowing the user to alternate between forms to create a sense of irregular movement. The result is a typeface that reads as unstable and fluid rather than static, suited to expressive editorial, poster and branding contexts. Glyph count sits at 364 with broad Latin language support covering over 80 languages.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe GabyGaby as an extreme experimental typeface with erratic fluidity, noting that expressions of movement can be felt across the family. Each letter has two different styles accessible via upper and lowercase keys.

name: Clarke
classification_notes: A condensed sans-serif with rounded corners and oblique rather than true italic styles, placing it in the grotesque tradition with a soft, geometric touch.
summary: A condensed sans-serif with rounded corners by Dominik Krotscheck, offered in four weights with matching obliques and a set of OpenType stylistic alternates.
description: Eight styles span Light through Black in both upright and oblique cuts, all sharing the same condensed proportions and softened corners that give the family a compact but approachable character. OpenType features include six stylistic sets offering alternate forms for a, g, k, t, J and Q, plus stylistic ligatures for added personality. Language support covers a broad range of Latin-based scripts across Europe and Africa.
foundry_blurb: Clarke is described by The Designers Foundry as a proud condensed sans-serif with rounded corners, available in four weights with regular and oblique styles for a total of eight fonts. OpenType features include alternative letterforms, alternative dieresis dots and stylistic ligatures, with support for over 70 languages.

name: Sideshow
classification_notes: Inspired directly by historic sign painting traditions around Los Angeles, Sideshow is a display typeface with vernacular lettering roots rather than any conventional type classification.
summary: A single-weight display face by letterer Drew Melton, drawn from historic sign painting traditions around Los Angeles.
description: Designed by Drew Melton, a Los Angeles-based graphic designer and letterer with clients including Nike and Penguin Books, it draws directly from vernacular sign painting references across the city. A Stylistic Set offers alternate letterforms, and the glyph set of 172 characters covers a broad range of Latin-based languages. Best suited to branding, packaging and editorial work where a hand-lettered Americana quality is wanted.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Sideshow as a sign painting-inspired typeface, with references drawn from various historic locations around Los Angeles. Designed by Drew Melton, it includes a stylistic alternate set and supports a wide range of Latin-script languages.

name: Strato
classification_notes: A humanist sans with proportions drawn from classical Roman inscriptions, but its primary design intent is layering across six decorative variants, placing it firmly in display territory rather than conventional text use.
summary: A six-style layered display sans by Sophie Brown, proportioned after classical Roman inscriptions and designed for mixing styles freely.
description: Six variants, Roman, Cesello, Inline, Outline, Eclisse and Tonale, each take their cue from a different texture of Rome: stone carvings, woodblock illustration and modern signage. Any combination of layers can be stacked to produce a range of decorative effects from a single underlying sans skeleton. The proportions draw from classical Roman letterforms but the overall character sits closer to contemporary display work than antiquarian revival.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Strato as a layered typeface inspired by the many facets of Rome, designed as a slightly humanist sans proportionally based on classical Roman inscriptions with a modern twist. Its six styles, Roman, Cesello, Inline, Outline, Eclisse and Tonale, each reflect different elements and textures of Rome, from old stone carvings to woodblock illustrations to modern signage, and can be freely combined for a near-endless variety of decorative results.

name: Dolcetto
classification_notes: Dolcetto is described as flowing from letter to letter with an air of opulence, placing it firmly in the connected script category designed for elegant, decorative use.
summary: A flowing connected script by letterer Drew Melton, offered in three weights with stylistic alternates for decorative and branding work.
description: Designed by graphic designer and letterer Drew Melton, Dolcetto is a connected script typeface positioned at the more formal, ornate end of the genre. Three weights from Light to Bold each include stylistic alternates, giving designers flexibility without straying from the face's calligraphic character. With 319 glyphs and broad Latin coverage spanning over 60 languages, it suits logotype work, packaging and event collateral where an air of refinement is needed.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Dolcetto as the epitome of elegance, a refined and grand typeface that flows effortlessly from letter to letter. It comes loaded with 330 glyphs across three weights, all including stylistic alternates, and is suited to projects requiring opulence and distinction.

name: Rothenburg
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as an awkward sans serif with odd angles, Rothenburg sits outside standard grotesque or geometric categories and functions as an experimental display sans.
summary: A small four-style experimental sans by Thomas Gillett, built around deliberately odd angles and an intentionally basic character set.
description: Designed by Australian type designer Thomas Gillett, Rothenburg pairs Regular and Bold weights with matching italics across a glyph count of just 179. The foundry frames it explicitly as an experiment, with off-kilter angles that sit uneasily but remain legible. Latin language support is broad, covering Afrikaans through to Zulu, though the limited character set makes it best suited to display and branding work rather than running text.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Rothenburg as an awkward sans serif with odd angles that somehow feel balanced, noting it has a basic character set as it is intended as an experiment in type design.

name: Superspitze
classification_notes: Explicitly inspired by bold geometric typefaces such as Futura, Erbar and Kabel, with slightly more humanist detailing; fits squarely within the geometric sans category.
summary: A bold geometric sans with humanist touches, offering an alternative stylistic set and decorative swash characters drawn from a near-century-old Schelter & Giesecke specimen book.
description: Designed by Patrick Martin, a graduate of Bauhaus University Weimar, this single-weight geometric sans sits between the precision of Futura and the warmth of more humanist designs. A secondary stylistic set simplifies forms for smaller text sizes, while a suite of Art Nouveau-influenced swash characters, taken from a Schelter & Giesecke specimen of around 100 years ago, provides decorative character suited to headlines and identity work. Language coverage spans 484 glyphs across a wide range of Latin-based scripts.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Superspitze as a bold geometric typeface inspired by Futura, Erbar and Kabel, with shapes that are slightly more humanist and detailed. An alternative stylistic set offers simpler forms for smaller sizes. Decorative swash characters, inspired by an Art Nouveau specimen from the German foundry Schelter & Giesecke, are included for headlines and identity projects, accessible via the glyphs panel in desktop software.

name: Gatefold
classification_notes: An editorial display serif with brush-like terminals and delicate serifs, developed within the Type@Cooper program. Its tall x-height and calligraphic sharpness place it firmly in the display serif tradition.
summary: A calligraphically influenced display serif by Scott Biersack, originating from the Type@Cooper Extended Program, offering six styles across three weights with matching italics.
description: Developed over nearly two years from the 2015-2016 Type@Cooper Extended Program in New York, this display serif carries sharp brush-like terminals, delicate serifs and a tall x-height suited to editorial headlines. Six styles span Book, Medium and Demibold with corresponding italics, supported by swash capitals, discretionary ligatures and a 906-glyph set. It has found real-world use with Harper Collins and Magnolia Bakery, pointing toward its comfort in premium editorial and branding contexts.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Gatefold as an elegant display typeface designed for editorial use, featuring sharp brush-like terminals, a tall x-height and delicate serifs. The family began in the 2015-2016 Type@Cooper Extended Program and was expanded over nearly two years to its current six styles, with OpenType features including swash capitals, ligatures, discretionary ligatures and fractions.

name: Selador
classification_notes: Selador is described as a calligraphic minimalist typeface drawing on early calligraphy letterforms, positioning it as a display face with expressive, alternative characteristics rather than a conventional text type.
summary: A single-weight calligraphic display face by Maximilian Huber, pitched at alternative and punk-adjacent graphic design work.
description: Drawn from early calligraphy letterforms but stripped back to a minimal aesthetic, Selador sits somewhere between expressive lettering and contemporary display type. It ships as a single Regular weight with a glyph count of 239 and broad Latin language coverage across African, European and Southeast Asian languages. Best suited to poster work, album art or editorial contexts where a raw, handmade energy is appropriate.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describes Selador as a modern and refined interpretation of early calligraphy letterforms, suited to alternative or punk style graphic design pieces, and classifies its aesthetic as calligraphic minimalism.

name: Ossature
classification_notes: Classified as a modern stencil typeface by the foundry; its squared forms with rounded corners and industrial signage origins place it firmly in the display/stencil category.
summary: A single-weight stencil face with squared geometry and softened corners, conceived for discreet signage in industrial building contexts.
description: Designed by Romain Bergaentzlé of studio OOII, Ossature draws on industrial renovation projects to produce a stencil typeface suited to understated signage systems. The squared shapes and rounded corners give it a considered, architecturally informed quality rather than the aggressive military stencil vernacular. With a glyph count of 229, it covers a broad range of Latin-script languages including most of Western and Central Africa.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Ossature as a modern stencil typeface combining squared shapes with rounded corners, designed for discreet signage systems and inspired by the renovation of industrial buildings.

name: Abraham
classification_notes: A single-weight hand-drawn display face with organic, illustrated letterforms and contextual alternates that simulate natural variation; firmly a display typeface rather than a text or script family.
summary: A hand-drawn display typeface by Jan Willem Wennekes with four contextual alternates per letter and optional retro interlocking discretionary ligatures.
description: Designed by Jan Willem Wennekes of Zeptonn, Abraham combines a soft, organic character with a confident stroke that makes it suitable for display headlines, packaging and branding. Each letter carries four variations that cycle automatically when contextual alternates are active, producing a more convincingly hand-lettered appearance. Discretionary ligatures add a retro interlocking effect, and the 564-glyph set covers a wide range of Latin-extended languages.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Abraham as a friendly hand-drawn display typeface whose organic letterforms are at once striking and soft. OpenType features include standard and discretionary ligatures, contextual alternates with four per-letter variations for a naturalistic hand-drawn effect, and ordinals with broad accented character support across numerous languages.

name: Tabi
classification_notes: Tabi is explicitly a display family, with a Super style that pushes letterforms toward illustrative, cryptic symbols, and a Regular that acts as a neutral counterpart. The extreme stylistic contrast and large-scale intent place it firmly in display.
summary: A two-style display family that pairs an extreme, symbol-like Super cut with a neutral Regular for contrast-driven typographic work.
description: Tabi offers two stylistically opposed cuts: the Super style pushes conventional letterforms toward abstract, illustrative shapes suited to large-scale and poster work, while the Regular provides a clean, neutral companion for mixing or grounding compositions. The family has been used by Apple Music, suggesting currency in commercial branding contexts. With 365 glyphs and broad Latin coverage across over 80 languages, it is practically equipped despite its expressive intent.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Tabi as a display family of two extremes. The Super style is disruptive and bold, creating a bridge between functional type and cryptic illustrative forms or symbols, intended for large-scale use. The Regular is neutral in aesthetic, designed to complement or mix with the Super style.

name: Wulkan Display
classification_notes: A modern high-contrast serif with old-style influences, characterised by thin hairlines, sharp exaggerated elements and ball terminals. Its compressed proportions and tight-setting capability place it firmly in the display serif tradition.
summary: A high-contrast display serif by Jan Estrada-Osmycki, drawing on old-style types and the work of Lubalin and Carnase for use at large sizes.
description: Designed between 2015 and 2019, it pairs thin hairlines and sharp, exaggerated strokes with ball terminals for a lively, vibrant character suited to headlines and branding. Slightly compressed proportions with short ascenders and descenders allow tight-setting for strong visual impact. Twelve styles span six weights from Light to Black with matching italics, plus three stylistic sets including slanted lowercase and alternate capitals.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Wulkan Display as a modern interpretation of old-style types, drawing inspiration from Charles Heyer, Herb Lubalin and Tom Carnase. They note its thin hairlines and exaggerated contrast against ball terminals, and highlight its compressed proportions and short extenders as features that allow tight setting for visual impact. This is noted as the legacy stand-alone version, distinct from the newer Wulkan family release.

name: Beival Sans
classification_notes: A tall, monospaced display face drawing on Art Deco geometric traditions from the 1920s and 1930s; its architectural proportions, ornamental glyph system and condensed verticality place it firmly in the display category rather than functional sans-serif.
summary: A tall monospaced display typeface by Guillaume Beinat Prunet, drawing on Art Deco poster and architectural lettering from 1920s Paris and New York.
description: Designed at ECV Bordeaux and released in two weights, the family channels geometric movements including Bauhaus, De Stijl and the Viennese Secession through its narrow, simplified forms and condensed bold. Beyond standard characters, it offers a system of decorative signs and overlapping glyphs intended to build structured ornamental compositions for headlines and editorial layouts. The 610-glyph count per style reflects broad Latin language coverage across European and African scripts.
foundry_blurb: The Designers Foundry describe Beival Sans as a tall, monospaced display typeface rooted in Art Deco aesthetics, drawing on poster and architectural typography from the Roaring Twenties and the Great Depression in Paris and New York. They highlight its dual role as a headline and ornamental system, noting that certain glyphs overlap in height to introduce rhythm and visual energy across titles and layouts.
